_meta.authoringNotes: Definition and diagnostic criteria per the Global Leadership Initiative on Malnutrition (GLIM) consensus (Cederholm T et al. Clin Nutr 2019, PMID 30181091) and ESPEN guidelines on clinical nutrition and hydration in geriatrics (Volkert D et al. Clin Nutr 2019, PMID 30005900). Epidemiology per Cereda E et al. Clin Nutr 2016 meta-analysis (PMID 26944653). Anorexia of aging per Morley JE. Am J Clin Nutr 1997 and subsequent reviews. Screening tools (MUST, MNA) per Stratton RJ et al. and Guigoz Y. Outcomes and intervention evidence per Milne AC et al. Cochrane 2009 (PMID 19370593) and the MaNuEL knowledge hub. Refeeding syndrome per NICE CG32 nutrition support guidance.

h1Title: Malnutrition in Older Adults: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: Malnutrition in older adults — most often undernutrition, meaning an inadequate intake or absorption of energy and protein — is a common, serious, and frequently overlooked condition. It affects an estimated 5-10% of community-dwelling older adults, around 20-30% of those admitted to hospital, and up to 50% of those in rehabilitation and long-term care. It is rarely a simple problem of not having food: it usually results from a combination of the 'anorexia of aging', acute and chronic illness, multiple medications, dental and swallowing problems, depression and cognitive impairment, social isolation, poverty, and reduced mobility. Malnutrition is not a benign accompaniment of old age — it is independently associated with loss of muscle and strength, falls, pressure injuries, impaired wound healing, weakened immunity and infection, longer hospital stays, loss of independence, admission to long-term care, and increased mortality. It is also closely intertwined with frailty and sarcopenia. The encouraging point is that it is detectable with simple screening tools and treatable: identifying at-risk older adults, finding and addressing the underlying causes, and providing food-first measures, fortified meals, oral nutritional supplements, and where needed enteral feeding can reverse weight loss, rebuild strength, and improve outcomes and quality of life.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects roughly 5-10% of community-dwelling older adults, 20-30% of hospital admissions, and up to 50% in rehabilitation and long-term care (Cereda Clin Nutr 2016)
keyStats.demographics: Risk rises with age, frailty, cognitive impairment, depression, multimorbidity, and social isolation; more common in those living alone and in residential care
keyStats.avgAge: Most common in adults over 75, with the highest rates in the very old and those in institutional care
keyStats.globalCases: One of the most common and under-recognized conditions of older age worldwide; a major contributor to the burden of frailty and disability

definition: Malnutrition in older adults (ICD-10: E40-E46 for protein-energy malnutrition; R63.6 for inadequate intake; R63.4 for abnormal weight loss) most commonly refers to undernutrition — a state resulting from a lack of intake or uptake of nutrition that leads to altered body composition and diminished function. The 2018 Global Leadership Initiative on Malnutrition (GLIM) consensus defines malnutrition by the presence of at least one phenotypic criterion — non-volitional weight loss, low body mass index, or reduced muscle mass — together with at least one etiologic criterion — reduced food intake or assimilation, or disease burden and inflammation. It is distinct from, but overlaps heavily with, two related geriatric syndromes: sarcopenia, the loss of muscle mass and strength, and frailty, a state of reduced physiological reserve. Older adults are particularly vulnerable because of the 'anorexia of aging' — a physiological decline in appetite and food intake with age driven by changes in appetite-regulating hormones, slower gastric emptying, reduced taste and smell, and altered satiety signaling — which leaves little reserve when illness, medication, dental problems, low mood, or social difficulty further reduce intake. Malnutrition can be primary (inadequate intake of otherwise normal requirements) or, very commonly in older adults, disease-related, where illness and inflammation both increase nutritional requirements and reduce intake. It is managed by geriatric medicine, primary care, dietetics, speech and language therapy, and nursing, ideally through routine screening and a structured, multidisciplinary nutritional care plan.

typesClassification.description: Inadequate intake of energy and protein in the absence of significant disease — driven by the anorexia of aging, social isolation, poverty, immobility, dental problems, or difficulty shopping and cooking.

Malnutrition driven by an acute or chronic illness — cancer, organ failure, infection, inflammatory disease — where inflammation increases requirements and suppresses appetite. Weight and muscle loss can occur despite seemingly adequate intake.

Malnutrition caused by a condition that mechanically or functionally limits intake or absorption — such as dysphagia after stroke, malabsorption, or severe dementia — without a major inflammatory component.

Malnutrition in which loss of muscle mass and strength (sarcopenia) is prominent, producing weakness, slow walking speed, falls, and functional decline. Malnutrition and sarcopenia frequently coexist and reinforce each other.

Deficiencies of vitamins and minerals — vitamin D, vitamin B12, folate, iron, zinc — which can occur alongside or independently of energy-protein undernutrition and contribute to anemia, bone disease, cognitive symptoms, and impaired healing.

Older adults who do not yet meet diagnostic criteria for malnutrition but score positive on screening tools, with recent reduced intake or early weight loss — the group in whom early intervention is most effective.

primarySymptoms: Unintentional weight loss — clothes, rings, and dentures becoming loose — which is the single most important warning sign.
Reduced appetite and reduced food intake, often with the person eating smaller portions or skipping meals.
Loss of muscle bulk and strength, with thinning of the arms, legs, and temples and difficulty with tasks such as rising from a chair or climbing stairs.
Fatigue, low energy, and reduced stamina.
Increased falls and unsteadiness from loss of muscle and strength.
Slow or poor wound healing and the development of pressure injuries.
Frequent or prolonged infections and slow recovery from illness, reflecting weakened immunity.
Low mood, apathy, irritability, and poor concentration, which can both cause and result from malnutrition.
Dry, thin, fragile skin, brittle nails, and hair thinning or loss.
Feeling cold, swelling of the ankles in severe protein deficiency, and a generally frail, unwell appearance.
Symptoms of specific micronutrient deficiencies — pallor and breathlessness from anemia, bone pain from vitamin D deficiency, or neurological symptoms from vitamin B12 deficiency.
earlyWarningSigns: A loose-fitting watch, ring, dentures, or clothes, indicating unnoticed weight loss
Eating noticeably less, skipping meals, or relying on a very limited range of easy foods
Reduced interest in food, cooking, or shopping, especially after bereavement or a change in circumstances
Becoming weaker, slower, or more prone to stumbling over a period of weeks to months
A positive result on a routine nutrition screening tool such as MUST or the Mini Nutritional Assessment
emergencySigns: Rapid, severe weight loss with marked weakness and inability to carry out basic daily activities
Signs of severe dehydration alongside poor intake — confusion, low blood pressure, reduced urine output
A new pressure injury, a non-healing wound, or signs of serious infection in a malnourished older adult
Choking, coughing during meals, or recurrent chest infections suggesting unsafe swallowing (aspiration)
Confusion, abnormal heart rhythm, or weakness developing soon after refeeding begins — possible refeeding syndrome, which is a medical emergency

causes.description: A physiological decline in appetite and food intake that occurs with normal aging, driven by changes in appetite-regulating hormones, earlier and stronger satiety, slower gastric emptying, and reduced taste and smell. It leaves little nutritional reserve when any additional stressor reduces intake further.

causes.cause: Acute and chronic illness with inflammation
causes.description: Cancer, heart failure, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, chronic kidney disease, infection, and inflammatory conditions both increase nutritional requirements and suppress appetite through inflammatory signaling, driving weight and muscle loss.

Poorly fitting dentures, missing teeth, mouth pain, dry mouth, and dysphagia (difficulty swallowing, often after stroke or in dementia and Parkinson disease) make eating difficult, slow, or unsafe and lead to reduced intake.
Many drugs reduce appetite, alter taste, cause nausea, dry mouth, or sedation, or impair nutrient absorption. Polypharmacy in older adults is a common, modifiable contributor to poor intake.

Depression reduces appetite and motivation to eat; dementia impairs the ability to shop, prepare food, recognize hunger, and complete a meal. Both are leading causes of malnutrition in older adults and are often unrecognized.
Living alone, social isolation, recent bereavement, poverty, difficulty shopping and cooking, reduced mobility, and lack of help with meals all reduce access to and intake of adequate food.

Conditions that impair digestion and absorption — coeliac disease, inflammatory bowel disease, pancreatic insufficiency, and previous gastrointestinal surgery — reduce the uptake of nutrients even when intake appears adequate.

riskFactors.factor: Advanced age and the anorexia of aging
riskFactors.description: The physiological decline in appetite and intake with age, most marked in the very old, reduces nutritional reserve and is the background on which other risk factors act.
Frailty and loss of muscle mass and strength both predispose to and result from malnutrition, creating a self-reinforcing cycle of decline.

riskFactors.factor: Acute or chronic illness and multimorbidity
riskFactors.description: Cancer, organ failure, infection, and the presence of several chronic conditions increase requirements and reduce intake, making disease-related malnutrition common.
Depression and dementia are leading, often unrecognized causes of poor intake; recognizing and treating them improves nutrition.
Poor dentition, mouth pain, dry mouth, and swallowing difficulty make eating hard or unsafe; dental care and swallowing assessment address these.
Multiple medications with appetite-suppressing, taste-altering, or absorption-impairing effects contribute to poor intake; medication review can reduce this.
Living alone, lack of social contact, recent loss of a partner, and limited income all reduce access to and enjoyment of food; social support and meal services help.

riskFactors.factor: Reduced mobility and difficulty shopping or cooking
riskFactors.description: Functional limitations that make it hard to obtain and prepare food are major practical contributors to malnutrition in community-dwelling older adults.

Acute illness, unfamiliar food, missed meals around tests and procedures, and inadequate help with eating make hospitals and care homes high-risk settings, where routine screening and feeding support are essential.

affectedDemographics: Malnutrition affects roughly 5-10% of community-dwelling older adults, with much higher rates in those who are frail, housebound, or recently bereaved.
Around 20-30% of older adults are malnourished on admission to hospital, and a substantial proportion deteriorate further during the stay.
Up to 50% of older adults in rehabilitation units and long-term care are malnourished or at risk.
Rates are higher in people with dementia, depression, stroke, cancer, and multiple chronic conditions.
Older adults living alone, on low incomes, or with limited mobility and social support are disproportionately affected, and the very old (over 85) are at the highest risk.
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of malnutrition in older adults begins with routine screening, because the condition is common, serious, and frequently missed — especially in people who are overweight, where weight loss and muscle loss can be overlooked. Validated screening tools such as the Malnutrition Universal Screening Tool (MUST) and the Mini Nutritional Assessment (MNA) are quick, can be used by any trained healthcare worker, and should be applied at primary care reviews, on hospital admission, and regularly in care homes. A positive screen triggers a full nutritional assessment. Diagnosis is then made using the Global Leadership Initiative on Malnutrition (GLIM) criteria, which require at least one phenotypic criterion (unintentional weight loss, low body mass index, or reduced muscle mass) and at least one etiologic criterion (reduced food intake or absorption, or disease burden and inflammation). Assessment also includes a thorough history of the causes — appetite, recent changes, dental and swallowing problems, mood, cognition, medications, social circumstances, mobility, and any underlying illness — and a focused examination for muscle wasting, fluid status, pressure areas, and signs of specific deficiencies. A swallowing assessment by a speech and language therapist is arranged when dysphagia is suspected. Targeted blood tests look for anemia, micronutrient deficiencies (vitamin D, vitamin B12, folate, iron), and markers of inflammation and organ function; it is important to recognize that albumin is a marker of inflammation and illness severity rather than a reliable measure of nutritional status on its own. Where weight loss is unexplained, investigation for an underlying cause such as cancer, malabsorption, hyperthyroidism, or chronic infection is needed. The central principle is that diagnosing malnutrition is only the first step — the assessment must also identify why the person is malnourished, because effective treatment depends on addressing those specific causes.

diagnosticTests.test: Validated nutrition screening (MUST or Mini Nutritional Assessment)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies older adults who are malnourished or at risk so that a full assessment and care plan can follow
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A short tool combining recent weight change, body mass index, and the effect of acute illness or reduced intake; takes a few minutes and can be done in any healthcare setting.

diagnosticTests.test: Weight, height, and body mass index with weight-history review
diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents low body mass index and quantifies unintentional weight loss, key phenotypic criteria for malnutrition
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Measurement of weight and height, calculation of body mass index, and a review of the rate and amount of any weight loss; serial weights track progress.

diagnosticTests.test: Assessment of muscle mass and strength
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects the reduced muscle mass of malnutrition and the overlapping syndrome of sarcopenia
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Practical bedside measures such as calf circumference, grip strength, and timed chair-rise or gait speed; more detailed body composition testing is used where available.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causes of poor intake — appetite, dental and swallowing problems, mood, cognition, medications, social and financial circumstances
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A structured history, often with a food diary, and review of all medications and the person's living and support situation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects dysphagia and unsafe swallowing, which both cause malnutrition and risk aspiration
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A bedside swallowing assessment by a speech and language therapist, with instrumental testing (videofluoroscopy or endoscopic evaluation) where needed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects anemia, micronutrient deficiencies, inflammation, and organ dysfunction, and helps investigate unexplained weight loss
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw including full blood count, vitamin D, vitamin B12, folate, iron studies, and inflammatory markers; results within days. Albumin reflects illness and inflammation rather than nutrition alone.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of malnutrition in older adults follows a structured, stepwise, multidisciplinary approach, and its success depends on combining nutritional support with treatment of the underlying causes. The first principle is to identify and address what is driving the poor intake — treating depression, optimizing dental care, managing dysphagia with texture-modified diets and swallowing therapy, reviewing and reducing appetite-suppressing medications, controlling pain and nausea, treating underlying illness, and arranging practical help with shopping, cooking, and eating. Nutritional support itself is delivered in steps. The 'food-first' approach comes first: encouraging frequent small meals and nourishing snacks, fortifying ordinary foods with added energy and protein (extra butter, cream, milk powder, cheese, and oils), offering preferred and familiar foods, making mealtimes social and unhurried, and providing physical assistance with eating where needed. When food-first measures are insufficient, oral nutritional supplements — energy- and protein-dense drinks — are added between meals; meta-analyses show these increase intake and weight and, in at-risk older adults, reduce complications and mortality. When intake remains inadequate and the gut is functional, enteral tube feeding (nasogastric in the short term, or a gastrostomy for longer-term needs) is considered, guided by the person's overall prognosis, goals, and wishes; the decision is individualized and, in advanced dementia, the evidence does not support routine tube feeding. Parenteral (intravenous) nutrition is reserved for the rare situations where the gut cannot be used. Alongside feeding, resistance and balance exercise is essential, because nutrition without physical activity does not rebuild muscle effectively — combined nutrition and exercise is far more effective than either alone for restoring strength and function. Throughout, refeeding syndrome — a potentially fatal shift of electrolytes when feeding is restarted in a severely malnourished person — must be anticipated, with cautious reintroduction of nutrition, electrolyte monitoring and replacement, and thiamine supplementation. Treatment is monitored with serial weights, intake records, and functional measures, and goals are set realistically and in line with the person's overall situation and preferences.

medicalTreatments.name: Treatment of the underlying causes of poor intake
medicalTreatments.description: Treating depression, optimizing dental care, managing dysphagia, reviewing appetite-suppressing medications, controlling pain and nausea, treating underlying illness, and arranging social and practical support with food. The essential first step on which nutritional support depends.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Addressing reversible causes is the determinant of long-term success; nutritional support alone fails if the underlying drivers are not corrected

medicalTreatments.name: Food-first dietary fortification and mealtime support
medicalTreatments.description: Frequent small meals and nourishing snacks, fortification of ordinary foods with added energy and protein, preferred and familiar foods, social and unhurried mealtimes, and physical assistance with eating where needed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: The recommended first-line nutritional intervention; improves intake and weight in many older adults without the need for supplements

medicalTreatments.name: Oral nutritional supplements
medicalTreatments.description: Energy- and protein-dense drinks (and other formats) given between meals when food-first measures are insufficient. Best taken between rather than instead of meals so they add to overall intake.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Increase energy and protein intake and body weight, and in at-risk and hospitalized older adults reduce complications and mortality (Milne Cochrane 2009, PMID 19370593)

medicalTreatments.name: Micronutrient supplementation
medicalTreatments.description: Correction of identified deficiencies — vitamin D, vitamin B12, folate, iron, and others — which contribute to anemia, bone disease, cognitive symptoms, and impaired healing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Corrects specific deficiencies and their consequences; targeted to documented deficiency rather than given indiscriminately

medicalTreatments.name: Enteral tube feeding (nasogastric or gastrostomy)
medicalTreatments.description: Delivery of nutrition directly into the stomach or small bowel when oral intake remains inadequate and the gut is functional. The decision is individualized, guided by prognosis, goals, and the person's wishes; routine use is not supported in advanced dementia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Maintains nutrition in appropriately selected patients, for example during recovery from stroke; benefit depends heavily on the clinical context and goals of care

medicalTreatments.name: Combined nutrition and resistance exercise
medicalTreatments.description: Pairing nutritional support with progressive resistance and balance exercise, because adequate protein and energy intake is needed for exercise to rebuild muscle, and exercise is needed for nutrition to translate into strength and function.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combined nutrition and exercise is substantially more effective than either alone for rebuilding muscle mass, strength, and physical function in older adults

medicalTreatments.name: Refeeding syndrome prevention
medicalTreatments.description: In severely malnourished people, nutrition is reintroduced cautiously with close monitoring and replacement of phosphate, potassium, and magnesium, and thiamine supplementation, to prevent the dangerous electrolyte shifts of refeeding syndrome.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents a potentially fatal complication; an essential safety component whenever feeding is restarted in a severely malnourished older adult

surgicalOptions.name: Percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) tube placement
surgicalOptions.description: An endoscopically placed feeding tube through the abdominal wall into the stomach, for older adults who need longer-term enteral nutrition and in whom it is consistent with their prognosis and wishes — for example after a disabling stroke. A carefully individualized decision, not used routinely in advanced dementia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Provides reliable long-term enteral access in appropriately selected patients; outcomes depend on the underlying condition and goals of care

surgicalOptions.name: Dental and oral surgical treatment
surgicalOptions.description: Treatment of dental disease, extraction of painful or broken teeth, and provision of well-fitting dentures to remove a common mechanical barrier to adequate eating.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Improves the ability and comfort of eating and supports better intake when oral problems are a contributing cause

alternativeTreatments.name: Community meal and social eating programs
alternativeTreatments.description: Meal delivery services, lunch clubs, shared meals, and befriending schemes that address the social isolation, practical difficulty, and loss of enjoyment that drive malnutrition in community-dwelling older adults.

alternativeTreatments.name: Appetite-supporting environmental and behavioural measures
alternativeTreatments.description: Improving the eating environment, offering preferred foods, protected mealtimes free of interruptions, finger foods for people with dementia, and adequate time and assistance — low-cost measures that meaningfully improve intake.

whySeeSpecialist: Malnutrition is best identified through routine screening in primary care, hospital, and care homes, with referral to a dietitian for full assessment and a nutritional care plan whenever screening is positive. Geriatric medicine input is valuable for the older adult with malnutrition alongside frailty, multimorbidity, cognitive impairment, or functional decline, and speech and language therapy is essential when swallowing problems are suspected. Specialist input also guides the individualized, sometimes difficult decisions about tube feeding.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a service that screens routinely for malnutrition and has ready access to a registered dietitian, with geriatric medicine, speech and language therapy, and dental input available. In hospital and care home settings, choose those with recognized nutritional care standards, protected mealtimes, and feeding assistance. For complex decisions about tube feeding, a geriatrician or specialist team experienced in shared, goals-based decision-making is important.
hospitalCriteria: Routine malnutrition screening of all older patients on admission
Ready access to a registered dietitian for full assessment and care planning
Speech and language therapy for swallowing assessment
Protected mealtimes and trained staff to provide feeding assistance
Geriatric medicine input for malnutrition alongside frailty and multimorbidity
keyFacilities: Care homes with recognized nutritional care standards
preventionStrategies: Screen older adults routinely for malnutrition risk in primary care, on hospital admission, and regularly in care homes, so problems are caught early.
Monitor weight regularly and investigate any unintentional weight loss promptly rather than attributing it to age.
Maintain dental health, well-fitting dentures, and prompt treatment of mouth problems that make eating difficult.
Review medications regularly to reduce those that suppress appetite, alter taste, or cause nausea.
Recognize and treat depression and cognitive impairment early, as both are leading causes of poor intake.
Support older adults who live alone with practical help for shopping, cooking, and shared meals to counter isolation.
Encourage regular physical activity, including resistance exercise, to maintain appetite, muscle, and strength.
Use food-first fortification and nourishing snacks early when intake begins to fall, before significant weight loss occurs.
lifestyleModifications: Eat little and often — small, frequent, energy- and protein-rich meals and snacks rather than relying on three large meals.
Fortify everyday foods with extra butter, cream, full-fat milk, cheese, milk powder, and oils to boost energy and protein without increasing volume.
Make mealtimes social and unhurried, eating with others where possible.
Keep easy, appealing, ready-to-eat foods available for low-energy days.
Take any prescribed oral nutritional supplements between meals so they add to, rather than replace, food intake.
Combine good nutrition with regular resistance and balance exercise to rebuild muscle and strength.
Stay hydrated, and arrange practical help with shopping and cooking if these have become difficult.

recommended: Energy- and protein-dense foods at every meal and snack (eggs, dairy, meat, fish, beans, nuts, nut butters)
Foods fortified with added butter, cream, cheese, milk powder, and oils to increase calories in small portions
Frequent nourishing snacks between meals — full-fat yoghurt, cheese and crackers, milky drinks, puddings
Adequate protein spread across the day (older adults generally need about 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day, more during illness) to support muscle
avoid: Low-fat, low-calorie, and 'diet' foods, which are counterproductive in undernutrition
Filling up on low-energy foods and drinks such as tea, broth, and salad before nutritious food
Long gaps without eating and missed meals
Restrictive therapeutic diets unless genuinely necessary, as they often reduce intake and worsen malnutrition in older adults

exerciseGuidelines: Physical activity, and resistance exercise in particular, is an essential partner to nutrition — adequate protein and energy intake allows exercise to rebuild muscle, and exercise turns nutrition into strength and function. Progressive resistance training two to three times a week, together with balance and walking activity, improves muscle mass, strength, mobility, and appetite. Exercise should be individualized to the person's ability and supervised where there is significant frailty or fall risk. Nutrition without exercise rebuilds muscle poorly, and exercise without adequate nutrition can worsen energy deficit.
dailyManagement: Eat small, frequent, fortified meals and nourishing snacks through the day.
Take prescribed oral nutritional supplements between meals as directed.
Weigh regularly (for example weekly) and keep a record to track progress.
Do prescribed resistance and balance exercises to rebuild muscle and strength.
Take prescribed micronutrient supplements and treat underlying conditions as advised.
Accept practical help with shopping, cooking, and eating, and make mealtimes social where possible.
Report ongoing weight loss, swallowing difficulty, or loss of appetite to a healthcare professional promptly.
prognosis: Malnutrition in older adults is a serious condition with a major impact on outcomes, but it is also treatable, and prognosis improves substantially when it is recognized and managed. Untreated, it is independently associated with loss of muscle and strength, falls and fractures, pressure injuries, impaired wound healing, weakened immunity and more frequent infections, longer hospital stays, loss of independence, admission to long-term care, and increased mortality — and it accelerates the cycle of frailty and functional decline. The prognosis depends heavily on the underlying cause: malnutrition driven by a reversible problem such as depression, dental disease, a treatable illness, or social difficulty often improves well once that cause is addressed alongside nutritional support. Malnutrition driven by advanced, progressive disease such as end-stage organ failure or advanced cancer carries the prognosis of that disease, and here the goals of nutritional care shift toward comfort and quality of life. Across all settings, the evidence shows that structured nutritional support — particularly food-first measures and oral nutritional supplements combined with resistance exercise — increases weight and muscle, improves strength and function, reduces complications, and in at-risk and hospitalized older adults reduces mortality. The key determinants of a good outcome are early detection through routine screening, a thorough search for and treatment of the underlying causes, a multidisciplinary care plan, and the combination of adequate nutrition with physical activity. Recognizing malnutrition as a treatable condition rather than an inevitable part of aging is itself one of the most important steps toward a better prognosis.
recoveryTimeline: With food-first measures and oral nutritional supplements, improvements in energy intake and a halt to weight loss can be seen within weeks, and weight regain over weeks to months. Rebuilding muscle mass and strength is slower, taking months of combined nutrition and resistance exercise. Recovery of function — strength, walking, and independence — follows muscle gain and is gradual. The timeline depends heavily on the underlying cause: reversible causes allow steady recovery, while progressive illness limits it. Refeeding in severely malnourished people must be deliberately slow over the first one to two weeks to avoid refeeding syndrome.
complications: Loss of muscle mass and strength (sarcopenia), with weakness and reduced mobility.
Falls, fractures, and reduced ability to carry out daily activities.
Pressure injuries and impaired, delayed wound healing.
Weakened immunity with more frequent and more severe infections and slower recovery.
Worsening frailty and accelerated functional and cognitive decline.
Longer hospital stays, higher readmission rates, and increased need for long-term care.
Micronutrient deficiency complications — anemia, bone disease, neurological symptoms.
Refeeding syndrome — dangerous electrolyte shifts when feeding is restarted in severe malnutrition.

supportResources.name: British Association for Parenteral and Enteral Nutrition (BAPEN)
supportResources.description: UK organisation providing the MUST screening tool, malnutrition resources, and guidance for clinicians and the public.

supportResources.name: European Society for Clinical Nutrition and Metabolism (ESPEN)
supportResources.description: Professional body publishing the clinical nutrition guidelines for geriatric and disease-related malnutrition.

supportResources.name: American Society for Parenteral and Enteral Nutrition (ASPEN)
supportResources.description: US professional body with clinical resources and patient information on malnutrition and nutrition support.

supportResources.name: Age UK
supportResources.description: UK charity with practical advice and support on eating well in later life and addressing the social causes of malnutrition.

confusedWithConditions.name: Frailty Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Frailty is a state of reduced physiological reserve and vulnerability across multiple systems, while malnutrition specifically describes inadequate energy and protein intake or uptake. They overlap and reinforce each other — malnutrition is a major driver of frailty — but malnutrition is defined by nutritional intake and body composition, and frailty by overall reserve and resilience.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sarcopenia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sarcopenia is specifically the loss of muscle mass and strength, which can occur with normal aging, inactivity, and disease as well as from malnutrition. Malnutrition is the broader nutritional state of inadequate intake or uptake. Sarcopenia is one of the phenotypic features used to diagnose malnutrition, and the two frequently coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Geriatric Depression
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Depression in older adults reduces appetite and motivation to eat and is a leading cause of malnutrition, but it is a mood disorder, not a nutritional state. The two often coexist; treating the depression is part of treating the malnutrition, and unexplained weight loss should prompt screening for both.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cachexia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cachexia is a specific inflammation-driven wasting syndrome seen with advanced cancer, organ failure, and other serious illnesses, in which weight and muscle are lost despite nutrition because of the disease's inflammatory effects. Simple undernutrition responds to nutritional support, whereas cachexia responds only partially because the underlying disease drives the wasting.

relatedConditions.name: Frailty Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Malnutrition is a major driver of frailty, and the two reinforce each other in a cycle of decline.

relatedConditions.name: Sarcopenia
relatedConditions.relevance: Loss of muscle mass is both a feature of malnutrition and a closely linked geriatric syndrome; combined nutrition and exercise treats both.

relatedConditions.name: Falls in Elderly
relatedConditions.relevance: Malnutrition weakens muscle and strength and increases fall risk; nutritional support is part of fall prevention.

relatedConditions.name: Delirium in the Elderly
relatedConditions.relevance: Poor nutrition and dehydration contribute to delirium risk during acute illness in older adults.

relatedConditions.name: Geriatric Depression
relatedConditions.relevance: Depression is a leading, often unrecognized cause of reduced appetite and malnutrition in older adults.

faqs.question: What is malnutrition in older adults?
faqs.answer: Malnutrition in older adults usually means undernutrition — an inadequate intake or absorption of energy and protein that leads to loss of weight and muscle and to reduced function. It is common, serious, and often missed, and it is treatable rather than an inevitable part of aging.

faqs.question: What causes malnutrition in the elderly?
faqs.answer: It usually has several causes at once: the natural decline in appetite with age, acute and chronic illness, dental and swallowing problems, multiple medications, depression and dementia, social isolation, bereavement, poverty, reduced mobility, and difficulty shopping and cooking. It is rarely simply a lack of food.

faqs.question: What are the warning signs of malnutrition in older people?
faqs.answer: Key warning signs are unintentional weight loss — loose clothes, rings, or dentures — reduced appetite and food intake, loss of muscle and strength, fatigue, increased falls, slow wound healing, frequent infections, and low mood. A positive result on a screening tool such as MUST is also a warning sign.

faqs.question: How is malnutrition diagnosed in older adults?
faqs.answer: It starts with routine screening using tools such as MUST or the Mini Nutritional Assessment. A positive screen leads to full assessment, with diagnosis made using the GLIM criteria — at least one phenotypic feature (weight loss, low BMI, or low muscle mass) plus one cause (reduced intake or disease and inflammation).

faqs.question: Is malnutrition a normal part of aging?
faqs.answer: No. Although appetite naturally declines somewhat with age, malnutrition itself is not a normal or inevitable part of aging. It is a treatable medical condition, and unintentional weight loss in an older adult should always be investigated rather than accepted as expected.

faqs.question: How is malnutrition in older adults treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment combines addressing the underlying causes — depression, dental problems, dysphagia, medications, illness, social difficulty — with stepwise nutritional support: food-first fortification and snacks, then oral nutritional supplements, and tube feeding if needed. Resistance exercise is paired with nutrition to rebuild muscle.

faqs.question: What is the food-first approach?
faqs.answer: The food-first approach means improving nutrition using ordinary food before turning to supplements: frequent small meals and nourishing snacks, fortifying everyday foods with extra butter, cream, cheese, and milk powder, offering preferred foods, and making mealtimes social and unhurried with help to eat where needed.

faqs.question: Do oral nutritional supplements work?
faqs.answer: Yes. Oral nutritional supplements — energy- and protein-dense drinks taken between meals — increase intake and body weight, and in at-risk and hospitalized older adults they reduce complications and mortality. They work best added to, rather than instead of, normal meals.

faqs.question: What is the difference between malnutrition and frailty?
faqs.answer: Malnutrition is specifically about inadequate energy and protein intake or uptake. Frailty is a broader state of reduced physiological reserve and vulnerability across many systems. They overlap and reinforce each other — malnutrition is a major driver of frailty — but they are not the same condition.

faqs.question: Why does appetite decrease with age?
faqs.answer: This is called the anorexia of aging. It results from changes in appetite-regulating hormones, earlier and stronger feelings of fullness, slower stomach emptying, and reduced taste and smell. It leaves older adults with little nutritional reserve when illness, medication, or social problems further reduce intake.

faqs.question: Can malnutrition cause falls in older adults?
faqs.answer: Yes. Malnutrition causes loss of muscle mass and strength, which directly increases the risk of falls and fractures. This is one reason nutritional support, combined with resistance exercise, is an important part of fall prevention in older adults.

faqs.question: Should everyone with malnutrition have a feeding tube?
faqs.answer: No. Tube feeding is reserved for specific situations where oral intake remains inadequate and the gut works, and the decision is individualized based on the person's prognosis, goals, and wishes. In advanced dementia, the evidence does not support routine tube feeding, and food-first care is preferred.

faqs.question: What is refeeding syndrome?
faqs.answer: Refeeding syndrome is a dangerous shift of electrolytes — particularly phosphate, potassium, and magnesium — that can occur when nutrition is restarted in a severely malnourished person. It can cause heart and neurological problems and can be fatal, so feeding is reintroduced slowly with monitoring and electrolyte and thiamine replacement.

faqs.question: Can an overweight older person be malnourished?
faqs.answer: Yes. Malnutrition is about inadequate intake of energy and protein and loss of muscle, not just low body weight. An overweight older adult who has lost significant weight or muscle can be malnourished, and this is easily missed — which is why screening looks at weight change and muscle, not just body mass index.

faqs.question: How much protein do older adults need?
faqs.answer: Healthy older adults generally need about 1.0-1.2 grams of protein per kilogram of body weight per day, more than younger adults, and requirements rise further during illness or recovery. Spreading protein across all meals and combining it with resistance exercise helps maintain and rebuild muscle.

faqs.question: Why is exercise important in treating malnutrition?
faqs.answer: Nutrition and exercise work together. Adequate protein and energy intake allows exercise to rebuild muscle, and resistance exercise is what turns that nutrition into strength and function. Nutrition without exercise rebuilds muscle poorly, so combined treatment is far more effective than either alone.

faqs.question: How can families help an older relative who is not eating well?
faqs.answer: Families can offer small, frequent, fortified meals and favourite foods, make mealtimes social and relaxed, help with shopping and cooking, watch for and report weight loss and swallowing problems, ensure dentures fit and the mouth is comfortable, and raise concerns with the doctor so underlying causes are checked.

faqs.question: Does malnutrition affect recovery from illness?
faqs.answer: Yes, considerably. Malnutrition weakens immunity, slows wound healing, increases the risk of pressure injuries and infections, prolongs hospital stays, and raises the risk of complications and death. Treating it improves recovery, which is why screening on hospital admission is standard.

faqs.question: What blood tests are used for malnutrition?
faqs.answer: Blood tests look for anemia, micronutrient deficiencies (vitamin D, vitamin B12, folate, iron), inflammation, and organ function, and help investigate unexplained weight loss. Importantly, albumin reflects illness and inflammation rather than being a reliable measure of nutrition on its own.

faqs.question: Can malnutrition in older adults be prevented?
faqs.answer: Yes, much of it can. Routine screening, monitoring weight, maintaining dental health, reviewing medications, recognizing and treating depression, supporting people who live alone with meals and company, encouraging exercise, and using food-first fortification early all help prevent malnutrition before significant weight loss occurs.

metaTitle: Malnutrition in Older Adults: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Malnutrition in older adults: causes of weight and muscle loss, screening with MUST, food-first care, oral nutritional supplements and exercise reviewed.
